extra.reasoning: rec_drug_a is set true only by r3 (priority 50), whose condition is the conjunction diabetes AND prior_gi_bleed AND anticoagulated AND bradycardia AND elderly AND seizure_history. rec_drug_b is set true only by r1 (priority 50), whose condition is diabetes. No rule ever sets rec_drug_a or rec_drug_b to false, and conflict resolution by priority only arbitrates among rules writing the SAME output; r1 writes rec_drug_b and r3 writes rec_drug_a, so they cannot suppress each other. Crucially, r3's condition contains diabetes as a conjunct, so any input that fires r3 (making rec_drug_a true) necessarily also fires r1 (making rec_drug_b true). The mutual-exclusion property (never rec_drug_a AND rec_drug_b jointly true) is therefore violated. Concrete counterexample: set all six of r3's conjuncts true (diabetes, prior_gi_bleed, anticoagulated, bradycardia, elderly, seizure_history) and the remaining inputs false. Then r3 yields rec_drug_a=true and r1 yields rec_drug_b=true, so both are true simultaneously.
